coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots based on cannabis seeds for efficient wound healing in a mouse incisional wound model: Link with stress and neurobehavioral effect

coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are promising biomaterials with potential applicability in several areas due to their many useful and unique features. Among different applications, GQDs are photodynamic therapy agents that generate single oxygen and improve antimicrobial activity. In the present study, and for the first time, GQD were isolated from the Cannabis sativa L. seeds to generate C-GQDs as a new biomaterial for antibacterial and wound healing applications. Detailed characterization was performed using FTIR, UV–vis, Raman spectra, photoluminescence, TEM examination, HRTEM, ζ-potential, and XRD. Our results revealed in vitro and in vivo antibacterial activity of C-GQDs against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) with reduced minimal inhibitory concentration of 236 µg/mL for both strains. In addition, the C-GQDs confirmed the in vitro analysis and exhibited anti-inflammatory activity by reducing the level of neutrophils in blood and skin tissue. C-GQDs act by accelerating re-epithelization and granulation tissue formation. In addition, C-GQDs restored neurobehavioral alteration induced by incisional wounds by reducing oxidative stress, decreasing cortisol levels, increasing anxiolytic-like effect, and increasing vertical locomotor activity. The wound-healing effects of C-GQDs support its role as a potential therapeutic agent for diverse skin injuries.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration for the synthesis process of C-GQDs from cannabis seeds as precursor using the microwave method.
A) FTIR spectrum, B) XRD spectrum, C) Raman spectra, D) Photoluminescence emission spectrum, and E) UV–visible spectra of C-GQDs.
A) TEM and B) HRTEM images of C-GQDs.
In vitro antibacterial effect of C-GQDs.

Antioxidant activity in bacterial suspensions after ROS production. Levels of SOD (A) and CAT (B) enzymes activity. Data presented in mean ± SEM, in which: * p < 0.05 vs. S. aureus; #p < 0.05 versus E. coli.
Evaluation of vertical locomotor activity in wounded and treated groups.Data presented as mean ± SEM, in which: * p < 0.05 versus Control; #p < 0.05 versus untreated.
Evaluation of anxiogenic effect in an incisional wound and anxiolytic effect of C-GQDs. Data presented as mean ± SEM, in which: * p < 0.05 versus Control; #p < 0.05 versus untreated.

Evaluation of time spent in the corner as a parameter of the anxiogenic effect in wounded mine and anxiolytic effect of GQDs. Data presented as mean ± SEM, in which: * p < 0.05 versus Control; #p < 0.05 versus untreated.
Photomicrographs of skin sections stained by H&E: control, untreated, C-GQDs, and povidone-iodine tissue at 1, 3, 6, and 9 days post-wounding.
Speed wound closure in control, untreated incisional wound model, C-GQDs, and povidone-iodine tissue at 1, 3, 6, and 9 days post-wounding.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are promising biomaterials with potential applicability in several areas due to their many useful and unique features. Among different applications, GQDs are photodynamic therapy agents that generate single oxygen and improve antimicrobial activity. In the present study, and for the first time, GQD were isolated from the Cannabis sativa L. seeds to generate C-GQDs as a new biomaterial for antibacterial and wound healing applications. Detailed characterization was performed using FTIR, UV–vis, Raman spectra, photoluminescence, TEM examination, HRTEM, ζ-potential, and XRD. Our results revealed in vitro and in vivo antibacterial activity of C-GQDs against Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli) with reduced minimal inhibitory concentration of 236 µg/mL for both strains. In addition, the C-GQDs confirmed the in vitro analysis and exhibited anti-inflammatory activity by reducing the level of neutrophils in blood and skin tissue. C-GQDs act by accelerating re-epithelization and granulation tissue formation. In addition, C-GQDs restored neurobehavioral alteration induced by incisional wounds by reducing oxidative stress, decreasing cortisol levels, increasing anxiolytic-like effect, and increasing vertical locomotor activity. The wound-healing effects of C-GQDs support its role as a potential therapeutic agent for diverse skin injuries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Wound repair is a worldwide medical issue implicating a number of highly controlled and complex reactions triggered by tissue break down to restore the functionality and integrity of affected tissues (Xu et al., 2020). Different kinds of wounds, including pressure, vascular, and diabetic ulcers, affect between 2.4 and 4.5 million people in the United States (Ahmed, 2022). An estimated 2 million patients are treated for acute or chronic wounds in Europe (Frydrych et al., 2019). Chronic wounds often represent a complication of chronic illnesses like diabetes (Beyene et al., 2020; Lindholm and Searle, 2016). Chen et al. have reported that the 5-year mortality rate in diabetic foot ulcers is comparable to cancer deaths (Armstrong et al., 2020). Chronic wounds significantly decrease the quality of life and add to the medical pressure (Olsson et al., 2019). Wound healing is a complicated and dynamic process involving hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation, remodeling, and bacterial infection may hinder these processes (Deng, 2022; Liang et al., 2021). Microorganisms [especially Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli)] can compete with the host immune system to invade injured tissues, causing infection, prolonged inflammation, and severe tissue damage (Ciofu, 2022). As a result of their elevated mortality and morbidity, diseases associated with bacterial infections have emerged as major public health issues.

The healing of skin wounds goes through four main steps, including hematoma formation, inflammation, proliferation, and tissue remodeling (Kaur et al., 2022; Wang, 2022; El Ayadi et al., 2020). These four steps overlap considerably and are supported by different mediators including immune cells, fibroblast, pro- and anti-inflammatory cytokines, and growth factors that contribute to new tissue formation (Kaur et al., 2022; Venugopal et al., 2023). Advanced biomaterials were shown useful in all stages of wound healing by removing wound exudate, providing wound coverage, transporting oxygen to the wound site, and preventing infection (Kaur et al., 2022; Yu et al., 2022). In addition, advanced biomaterials act as transport vehicles for proteins, drugs, growth factors, and antimicrobial agents (Kaur et al., 2022; Yu et al., 2022).

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are advanced biomaterials and potential candidates for biomedical applications, owing to their solubility in aqueous solutions and high biocompatibility (Abbas et al., 2018; Iravani and Varma, 2020). GQDs are nanostructures of graphene in the size range of 2–20 nm with a set of unique chemical and physical properties (Haque et al., 2018). Photostable GQDs can absorb a suitable wavelength for photon energy and transfer to specific activation, then generate heating effects and/or reactive oxygen species (ROS), known as cytotoxic agents in phototherapy (Zhang et al., 2019; Kumar, 2022). GQDs were shown efficient when treating various wounds and inflammatory conditions because of their low toxicity, non-invasive nature, and flexibility. In general, GQDs have no apparent toxicity, they have demonstrated a high potential for utilization in cellular imaging, as antibacterial material, and in drug delivery (Sangam et al., 2018; Cui et al., 2021; Henna and Pramod, 2020). To meet industrial needs, it is crucial to produce GQDs on a large scale with cost-effectiveness, adaptability, and environmental friendliness in mind.

The focus on sustainable and eco-conscious materials derived from natural origins has witnessed a remarkable upswing, primarily owing to their inherent traits such as recyclability, biodegradability, compatibility, and non-toxicity (Khalid et al., 2021). Materials sourced from plants are valuable resources for eco-friendly GQD synthesis, given their higher carbon content ideal for GQD production (Saleem et al., 2023). Kumawat et al. utilized GQDs produced from mango leaves using an eco-friendly approach for near-infrared bioimaging applications (Kumawat et al., 2017). The same research team employed grape seed extracts as a green carbon source for GQD synthesis (Kumawat et al., 2017). Teymourinia et al. employed corn powder as a precursor for the green chemistry-based synthesis of GQDs (Teymourinia et al., 2017). In another study, GQDs crafted from Clitoria Ternatea flowers via a one-pot microwave-assisted green synthesis method were used as an adjuvant for the treatment of Alzheimer's disease (Tak et al., 2020). Recently, GQDs were derived from date palm leaf powder, exhibiting sizes ranging from 3.5 to 8 nm, allowing for large-scale production that can be available for biomedical applications (Saleem et al., 2023). The outcomes of these aforementioned studies inspired our investigation into the potential to develop GQDs from renewable, cost-effective, natural, and sustainable sources such as green plants.

In the present work, Cannabis sativa L. seeds GQDs (termed here as C-GQDs) were generated through a novel eco-friendly approach using cannabis seeds as precursor and without the addition of strong oxidants, thus avoiding the production of toxic gases. As shown in Fig. 1 , C-GQDs were synthesized by the simple microwave approach. Cannabis seeds offer an opportunity in regard to versatility, cost, and availability. They are a rich source of fiber and have significant medicinal value (Rashid, 2021). They contain antibacterial cannabinoids with the potential to kill antibiotic-resistant bacteria (Farha et al., 2020). They also possess analgesics and anti-inflammatory effects that can be used in various biomedical applications (Tiwari et al., 2019; Balant, 2021; Raina, 2020). The as-synthesized C-GQDs were comprehensively characterized and systematically evaluated. In vivo and in vitro results revealed that the C-GQDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility and significantly augmented capability for killing both S. aureus and E. coli with a minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) of 236 µg/mL for both strains compared with povidone-iodine alone. More importantly, we found that C-GQDs accelerate the healing process by killing S. aureus and E. coli implicated in skin wound infection. The antibacterial and anti-inflammatory activities of C-GQDs against S. aureus and E. coli were demonstrated by increased neutrophil levels in serum and wounded tissue. The neurobehavioral impairments of incisional wounds including stress and anxiogenic-like effect were evaluated using the open-field behavioral test and serum cortisol level quantification. A clear anxiolytic-like effect with increased vertical locomotor activity in C-GQDs treated mice was confirmed by reduced cortisol levels implicated in wound healing impairment. The C-GQDs, via their antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, anti-stress, anxiolytic-like effects showed an accelerative potential of wound closure in mice models of incisional wounds.

Cannabis seeds were obtained from the Chefchaouen region in North Morocco. Ethanol, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA), phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), trichloroacetic acid (TCA), thiobarbituric acid (TBA), malondialdehyde (MAD), riboflavin, and diethyl ether were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany).

The cannabis seeds (15 g) were washed with deionized water and then oven-dried at 60 °C. After that the seeds were milled using High-Speed Electric Grain Grinder Cereal Mill Flour Powder Machine 110 V, then the powder was suspended in absolute ethanol (70 mL), The mixture was kept under constant stirring for 3 h and then centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 11 min. Afterward, the green extract was filtered using Whatman filter paperwith a pore size of 45 µm and the solvent was evaporated using a Rotary evaporator until a residual slurry was obtained. The slurry was blended with a minimal amount of milli-Q water and then heated at 700 W in a household microwave oven for 7 to 15 min. Subsequently, the resulting product was dispersed in absolute ethanol to ensure the even distribution of C-GQDs. This dispersion was then passed through a 0.22 μm syringe filter to isolate the pure C-GQDs. The C-GQDs were then dried at 65 °C for 24 h, resulting in a final product of dry powder. Finally, C-GQDs were placed in borosilicate vials at 4 °C.

The crystalline structures of C-GQDs were characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8-ADVANCE, Bruker, Karlsruhe, Germany) with a Cu-Kα radiation source (λ = 0.15418 nm) at 35 kV and a counting rate of 2°/min in the 5° to 60° scanning angle range. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was performed using LIBRA 120 PLUS de Carl Zeiss SMT, operating at various accelerating voltages ranging from 80 y 120 KV, with prealineaments at 80 kV and 200 kV. This allowed for a systematic investigation of GQD morphology and structural consistency across a range of voltages. Additionally, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) analysis was conducted utilizing a TALOS F200C G2 operating at 20 to 200 kV with pre-alignments at 80 kV and 200 kV. This microscope offers a point-to-point resolution of 0.19 nm, allowing for precise imaging at the nanoscale. C-GQDs were dispersed onto a carbon-coated copper grid and examined under vacuum conditions (1 x 10^-6 Torr). The UV–Vis absorption spectra of the samples were obtained using a UV–Vis spectrometer (Perkin Elmer Lamda-25). The photoluminescence (PL) spectrum emission spectra were determined with a fluorescent spectrophotometer (RF-6000, Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan) with a xenon lamp as an excitation source was used to record the PL spectra of the C-GQDs. The Raman spectra were measured with a confocal micro-Raman spectrophotometer (Bruker optics, Ettlingen, German) and excited at the wavelength of 532 nm. The power of the laser used on the sample was 0.20 mW. A Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR) was employed to record FTIR spectra ranging from 4000 to 400 cm−1 (Perkin-Elmer Spectrum Two, Waltham, MA, USA). The ζ-potential was investigated employing a c- Malvern Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern Instruments, UK).

The synthesized C-GQDs were evaluated for their antibacterial activity against different types of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria obtained from American Type Culture Collection (ATCC): S. aureus ATCC 25923, Pseudomonas aeruginosa ATCC 27853, E. coli ATCC 25922 and Streptococcus faecium ATCC 12755. Other types of bacteria obtained from the Provincial Hospital of Tetouan-Morocco (PHTM) were used: Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus spp, Extended-Spectrum Beta-Lactamase (ESBL) E. coli, klebsiella pneumonia, Acinetobacter baumannii, and Acinetobacter spp. The types of bacteria used in the present study are implicated in different dermatological pathologies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial test by gel diffusion method
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the antibacterial activity of C-GQDs, we used the disk diffusion method by mid-agar [Mueller Hinton (MH)] on Petri dishes (Galani et al., 2008). Following the method of Vincent (Aromatogram), a few milliliters of the bacterial strain and the media surface were inoculated. C-GQDs nanomaterial was dissolved in 1 % DMSO and applied to the surface agar containing the bacterial strain and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. The antibacterial effect was observed by the formation of the inhibition zone around the discs containing the C-GQDs nanomaterial and quantified by measuring the diameter of the inhibition zone.

The C-GQDs were used to measure the MIC and minimal bactericide concentration (MBC) (Supplementary material, Table 1S). The microtiter plates method was adopted using MTT. Liquid culture medium MH (100 µL) and liquid C-GQD nanomaterials (100 µL) were mixed, and the serial dilutions were prepared. 10 µL bacterial suspension was inoculated in each well and incubated at 37 °C. After 24 h, 10 µL of MTT (0.4 mg/mL) was added to each well and re-incubated for 30 min at 37 °C. The development of a blue-violet color was observed in wells with obvious microbial growth. The MIC value was then determined as the lowest concentration of C-GQDs nanomaterial with no bacterial growth.

The experiment aimed to assess the antibiotic effect of C-GQDs on strains of E. coli and S. aureus. To begin, bacterial suspensions containing 105 to 107 colony-forming units per milliliter (CFU/mL) (OD600 = 1) of both bacterial strains were prepared. These suspensions were separately cultured in five different media, each containing 100 mL of liquid nutrient broth. The media included: C1 (nutrient broth with E. coli), C2 (nutrient broth with S. aureus), C3 (nutrient broth with E. coli and C-GQDs), and C4 (nutrient broth with S. aureus and C-GQDs). Subsequently, the cultures were incubated in a shaker incubator set at 150 rpm and 37 °C for 2 days to allow for bacterial growth and C-GQDs interaction. Following the incubation period, 15 mL aliquots from each culture were extracted and subjected to centrifugation to isolate the bacterial cells and C-GQD complexes. The extracted samples were then analyzed to assess the levels of total proteins and stress enzymes present (Lazareva and Keller, 2014).

To determine whether C-GQDs exert antibacterial effects on E. coli and S. aureus through oxidative stress, we examined oxidative stress stabilizing enzymes such as superoxide dismutase (SOD) and catalase (CAT) activity. Roughly 10 mL of bacterial culture underwent a cold harvest and was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min at 4 °C. The resulting biomass was washed with PBS at pH 7.0, followed by another centrifugation and the resulting pellet was suspended in PBS. To break down single cells, ultrasonic disintegration was employed using a 30-second cycle repeated for a total of 5 min, with intermittent 1-minute cool-down periods at room temperature. This was followed by centrifugation at 12000 rpm for 15 min to effectively remove cellular debris. The obtained supernatants were dialyzed in potassium phosphate buffer and used for the enzyme assay.

The activity of SOD (EC 1.15.1.1) was assessed by gauging the photochemical reduction capacity of nitro blue toluene (NBT) as per Dhindsa et al.'s method (Dhindsa et al., 1981). Test tubes containing 1.5 mL of buffer, 0.2 mL of methionine, 0.1 mL of the supernatant combined with equal portions of NaCO3, NBT, riboflavin, EDTA, and distilled water were subjected to fluorescent illumination at 25 °C within a laminar airflow cabinet. Subsequent to illumination, absorbance was measured at 560 nm. The SOD activity denotes in the units, i.e., the amount of enzyme that inhibits the NBT photoreduction by 50 %, and the enzyme was quantified on the basis of percent inhibition (Pandey et al., 2013).

The CAT activity was determined by measuring the rate of decrease in absorbance at 240 nm of 12.5 mM H2O2 solution in 50 mM K-phosphate (pH 7.0) at 30 °C (Choudhary et al., 2012). The amount of enzyme per assay was adjusted so that the rate of reaction was kept linear for 2 min. One unit is defined as the amount of enzyme catalyzing the decomposition of 1 M of H2O2 per minute calculated from the extinction coefficient of H2O2 at 240 nm of 0.036 cm2 l mol−1.

0.5 mL of bacterial suspension samples were incubated for 4 h with 0.5 mL of C-GQDs (10 mg/mL). These incubations were stopped by employing 1 mL ofTCA 35 % (p/v) in the absence of light. 1 mL of 0.5 %TBA was then added, and the samples were heated to 80 °C for 30 min. The samples were then cooled in an ice bath before centrifugation, and the absorbance of the supernatant was determined at 535 nm. A calibration curve of MDA solutions, a lipid peroxidation marker, were applied to estimate the lipid oxidation, with MDA levels expressed per mg of protein (nmol MDA/mgP) (Páez et al., 2011).

The animal facility at Abdelmalek Essaâdi University of Tetouan (Morocco), provided male mice aged 12 to 15 weeks, weighing 30 to 33 g. Animals were housed in polycarbonate cages with 12-hour dark/light cycles at a temperature of 23 ± 1 °C and unlimited access to food and drink. Mice were randomized to four treatment groups (N = 40 each); Group 1 (Control-non-injured), and injured groups: Group 2 (Injury-untreated), Group 3 (Injury-Povidone Iodine), and Group 4 (Injury-C-GQDs). Animal studies were conducted in compliance with European legislation governing the moral assessment and authorization of initiatives involving the use of animals, February 1st, 2013, NOR: AGRG1238767A. In addition, every effort was made to reduce the number of mice utilized and minimize suffering.

Male mice (30–32 g, 12–15-week-old) were obtained from the principal animal house at Abdelmalek Essaâdi University of Tetouan. All animal experimental procedures were accomplished in the physiology and physiopathology laboratory of the University Abdelmalek Essaâdi of Tetouan. Mice were randomized into four treatment groups (N = 10 each per treatment per time point). The mice were anesthetized with diethyl ether and the back hair was shaved and 12 mm long full-thickness longitudinal incisions were created on the dorsal area.

Animals were separated into four groups: Control, Untreated, Treated with Povidone Iodine, and Treated with C-GQDs. Mice were anesthetized with diethyl ether and a longitudinal incisional wound of 12 mm diameter was created on the dorsum using sterile scissors. Treatments were applied daily starting 15 min after injury and once a day thereafter. The obtained C-GQD was dissolved in DI and a 100 μL of C-GQDs solution with a concentration of 100 μg/mL was applied to the incisional wound of the C-GQDs group. The 100 μg/mL was chosen based on our preliminary analysis of acute toxicity and safety of C-GQDs. The results of the safety and toxicity were analyzed via histological study, the biotoxicity of various concentrations of C-GQDs (0.10, 1.0, 10, and 20 mg/mL), in each group, samples of the left kidney and liver were analyzed using H&E analysis. Compared with samples of control mice (Figure 1S, Supplementary material), with the highest concentration (20 mg/mL) of C-GQDs no renal tissue alterations were observed with normal tubules. The glomeruli showed a normal tissue with no incomplete obliteration of the capsular space and no reduction of Bowman’s space was observed. In addition, histological analysis of the liver after exposure to the highest concentration (20 mg/mL) of C-GQDs, no signs of biotoxicity and disorganized tissue were observed (Table 2S and Figure 1S). The third group was treated with 100 μL of Povidone Iodine (10 % solution). To assess the healing process, photographs, and daily measurements of the wound size were taken. The mice were sacrificed after days 1, 3, 6, and 9 post-wounding, and tissues from the wound areas were collected for histopathological analysis.

Open-field test (OFT) was selected to investigate vertical locomotor activity and anxiety-like behavior after 1, 3, 6, and 9 days post-wounding (Tamegart, 2021). These studies were performed using an open field with a top-mounted camera. Animals were placed in the middle of the open field and given five minutes to roam freely. Rearing was used to measure vertical locomotor activity and the time spent in the central zone was used as a surrogate for anxiety-like behavior. To prevent the influence of residual odorant molecules between animals, the apparatus was cleaned with 70 % ethanol after each test.

After the experimental phase, the animals underwent an 8-hour fast before blood samples were obtained. Capillary tubing was used to extract blood from the orbital venous retrograde plexus of the eye on days 1, 3, 6, and 9 of the incisional wound, which was then stored in clean tubes containing calcium EDTA for the measurement of neutrophil levels using an auto hematology analyzer (BC-2800) (Al-Attar, 2022).

To identify the anxiogenic-like effect of incisional wounds and the anxiolytic-like and anti-stress effect of C-GQDs, blood samples were collected from the jugular vein of mice under anesthesia. The blood was centrifuged (3000 x g for 20 min at 4 °C), the serum isolated and prepared for biochemical analysis of cortisol levels.

After euthanasia, skin samples were collected on days 1, 3, 6, and 9 after wounding. Skin biopsies were fixed in 10 % formalin for 24 h and placed in plastic cassettes before processing and paraffinoma bedding. Skin tissues were cut into fine 4 µm sections using a semi-automatic microtome, stained with hematoxylin and eosin using established protocols. Afterward, the stained sections were imaged using an optical microscope coupled with a camera.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Wound contraction analysis and percentage of re-epithelialization
ce:section.ce:para: To examine the healing process and the wound repair, all groups were photographed using a camera, and the surface area of the wound was measured using ImageJ (Image J, NIH, USA) (Schneider et al., 2012). The percentage of re-epithelialization was quantified using ImageJ software. Briefly, re-epithelialization zones were outlined manually and measured using ImageJ analytical tools (Image J, NIH, USA) (Schneider et al., 2012). The data were expressed as percentage of re-epithelialization.

One-way ANOVA way was used to statistically evaluate the data. Post hoc differences between group means were examined using the Tukey test. P values less than 0.05 were deemed significant, and data were reported as mean ± Standard error of the mean (SEM). All statistical analyses were performed using SPSS 10.0 for Windows (SPSS, IBM, Chicago, IL, USA).

FTIR analysis was used to determine the functional groups on the surface of the as-prepared C-GQDs. Fig. 2
A illustrates the FTIR spectrum of C-GQDs and shows a broad peak at 3450 cm−1 attributed to stretching vibrations of hydroxyl bending (O–H). Another peak at 2938–2851 cm−1 represents the C–H stretching of the C–H bonding (conjugated skeleton of the C-GQDs). The presence of the C = O on the surface of C-GQDs was validated by the existence of the absorption band at 1610 cm−1. The spectrum of the C-GQDs curve also presents a band at a range of 1423 to 1358 cm−1 that was attributed to C-O–H stretching vibrations, the C–C vibrations of the epoxy group were identified by the bands at 929–882 cm−1, and the stretching vibrations of C-O-C are confirmed by the peak at 1210 cm−1. In addition, a C-O band is found at 1022 cm−1, which indicates that the C-GQDs were successfully synthesized (Chai et al., 2019; Mandani et al., 2017). X-ray diffraction patterns in Fig. 2 B show a typical XRD profile of the as-prepared C-GQDs. The patterns of XRD indicated a broad peak at (2θ) 22 Å attributed to the (002) peak, suggesting that cannabis produces graphene structures through the microwave reaction (Hassanpouraghdam et al., 2022; Chen et al., 2018). The Raman spectra of C-GQDs in the spectral range 1000 –1600 cm−1 are illustrated in Fig. 2 C (532 nm excitation). The spectra demonstrate typical graphitic characteristics including the D mode (∼1370 cm−1), activated by symmetry breaking at defects and edges, and the G-band (∼1500 cm−1), which arises owing to in-plane C–C deformations. The PL spectra of the C-GQDs at 430 excitation wavelengths are illustrated in Fig. 2 D. The spectra of the C-GQDs demonstrated an emission peak at approximately 430 nm, that is, a blue emission. Fig. 2 E shows the UV–vis, spectra of the prepared C-GQDs. The synthesized C-GQDs exhibit a broad peak in the region of about 250–280 nm attributed respectively to the π-π* electronic transition resulting from the sp2 aromatic ring (C = C) and n- π* electronic transitions (Centeno, 2021). C-GQDs were comprehensively characterized using TEM and HRTEM to elucidate their structural attributes. TEM and HRTEM images provided valuable information about the size and shape of the C-GQDs which confirmed the well preparation of C-GQDs (Fig. 3
A and 3B). We conducted ζ-potential measurements on C-GQDs to investigate the characteristics of their surfaces and their electron density. The ζ-potential of C-GQDs was determined to be −4.88 mV, indicating that all quantum dots possess negatively charged surfaces and the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups (Fig. 4 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial effect and antimicrobial mechanism of C-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The antibacterial activity of C-GQDs is shown in Fig. 5 . The obtained result shows the in vitro antibacterial effect of the C-GQDs by testing the MIC and MBC (Table 1S). In addition, the size of diffusion ring of C-GQDs were measured (19 mm) Figure 2S. We found that C-GQDs have a potent antibacterial effect against S. aureus and E. coli with a MIC of 236 µg/mL. The MBC concentrations of C-GQDs were also determined, the C-GQDs show an antibacterial activity with an MBC = 236 µg/mL against both S. aureus and E. coli. The sub-culturing results showed the absence of growth inhibition of C-GQDs in the four different bacteria used: Acinetobacterbaumannii), E. coli (ESBL), S. aureus (MLSB), Klebsiella pneumonia (ESBL), Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Streptococcus faecium, and Acinetobacter spp ( Fig. 5 ) . Previous studies revealed the antibacterial potential of Cannabis sativa L. seeds extract against different bacterial strains such as S. aureus and E. coli implicated in various dermatological pathologies (Raina, 2020; Frassinetti, 2020; Schofs et al., 2021). These findings supported our choice of Cannabis sativa L. seeds as a raw material for the synthesis of C-GQDs to test their effect on wound healing. The C-GQDs antibacterial effect against S. aureus and E. coli with reduced MIC value (236 µg/mL) was confirmed by visible light-activatable halogen/nitrogen co-doped polymeric GQDs (with best MIC values (0.05 µg/mL for S. aureus and 0.005 µg/mL for E. coli) after activation by visible light, the photodynamic therapy increases the ROS production (Huang et al., 2021). In addition, GQDs-coated silver nanoparticles (GQDs@AgNPs) have a potential antibacterial effect (11.4 µg/mL) against two bacterial strains (S. aureus and E. coli) (Zhong et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: C-GQDs reverse the behavioral effects of incisional wound
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of skin incision wounds on vertical motor activity was evaluated; the number of rearing was counted. The results in Fig. 7 show that after 1, 3, 6, and 9 days of the incisional wound, the number of rearings was significantly decreased in the untreated group compared with the control group. However, C-GQDs application restored rearing activity in the treated group and significantly increased the number of rearings to control levels. Excisional cutaneous wounds in mice were reported to decrease locomotor activity evaluated by the open-field test (Romana-Souza et al., 2010). Reduction in locomotor activity could be linked to the painful effects of incisional wounds. A previous study showed that a 5-mm longitudinal incision made on the dorsal skin of mice induced pain behaviors (Pogatzki and Raja, 2003; Huss et al., 2019). Behavioral tests such as the response to von Frey filaments and response to a radiant heat stimulus revealed painful effects in wounded animals (Pogatzki and Raja, 2003; Huss et al., 2019). Painful effects could be induced by toxins released by S. aureus in incisional wounds. A study revealed that S. aureus released α-hemolysin toxin, a pore-forming toxin that produces spontaneous pain behavior and forms pores in neuronal membranes, the same study revealed that S. aureus induced significant heat hyperalgesia (Blake et al., 2018). Another paper mentioned that S. aureus produces pain by direct activation of nociceptor sensory neurons, inducing calcium flux and action potentials (Chiu et al., 2013). The restorative effect of vertical locomotor activity in C-GQDs treated animals could be explained by their antibacterial effects. In addition, the C-GQDs also showed powerful antibacterial effects against S. aureus and decreased painful toxin levels in the wounded area (Huang et al., 2021; Karahan et al., 2018).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Anxiogenic-like effect of the incisional wound and anxiolytic-like effect of C-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Anxiogenic-like effect of incisional wounds and the anti-anxiolytic-like effect of C-GQDs were determined using the OFT. The results in Fig. 8 show that at 1, 3, 6, and 9 days after the incisional wound, the time spent in the central zone decreased significantly in the untreated group compared with the control. However, the anxiogenic-like effect of the incisional wound was reversed with C-GQDs application, and the time spent in the central zone was significantly increased. The anxiogenic-like effect of the incisional wound, evaluated with the time spent in the central zone, was confirmed by the time spent in the corners of the open field apparatus. The results in Fig. 9 show that incisional wounds increased the time spent in the corner in the untreated group compared with the control. The C-GQDs application decreased significantly the time spent in the corner compared with the injured non-treated group.

The OFT is an established test for the anxiolytic-like effect of nanomaterials based on the time spent in the central zone and corner, where avoidance of the central zone and increased time spent in corners were considered an anxiety response (Tamegart, 2021). The effects of incisional and excisional skin wounds on animal behavior are rarely studied. Our results are consistent with a previous study showing that skin injury can be associated with psychological disorders, such as anxiety-like behavior (Nguyen et al., 2022). The same study revealed that psoriasis-like skin lesions induced an obvious anxiogenic-like effect when using the elevated plus maze test (Nguyen et al., 2022). Incisional wounds can induce stress, leading to stress-related conditions (Stechmiller et al., 2019). A study reported anxiety in older individuals with chronic venous leg ulcers, and non-healing wounds in patients showed a correlation with psychoneurological symptoms including pain, anxiety, cognitive dysfunction, fatigue, and depression (Stechmiller et al., 2019). Additionally, the present findings suggest that C-GQD treatment had beneficial effects on anxiety-like behavior induced by incisional wounds.

The cortisol levels were significantly higher in the untreated group compared to control non-injured animals after 24 h. The C-GQDs group cortisol level was similar to the control non-injured group (Figure 5S). 3 days post-wounding, serum cortisol levels increased significantly in untreated, and C-GQD-treated animals compared with control animals. Serum cortisol was also higher in the povidone-iodine group compared to controls. 6 days post-wounding, an obvious anxiolytic-like effect was observed in the C-GQDs group with a significant reduction in serum cortisol compared to the untreated and povidone-iodine groups. Povidone iodine also reduced cortisol levels. Serum cortisol levels remain elevated by 9 days after injury in untreated animals compared to controls. These levels were reduced by C-GQDs and povidone-iodine treatments.

In this study, incisional wounds in mice exhibited anxiety-like effects and increased levels of serum cortisol than the unwounded mice. These results are consistent with other studies showing that skin wounds were paired with increased cortisol levels, stress, and anxiogenic effects. A previous study proposed a novel mechanism of action by which glucocorticoids (GC), including cortisol, impair wound healing by stimulating specific signaling pathways (β-catenin and c-myc) leading to delayed keratinocytes proliferation and migration and delayed re-epithelization (Slominski and Zmijewski, 2017). Skin wounds activate the hypothalamus–pituitary–adrenal axis, which leads to the secretion of the stress hormone cortisol, which is related to psychiatric disorders, including anxiety (Slominski and Zmijewski, 2017). The inhibition of 11βb-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 1 (11βHSD1) and stimulation of 11βb-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 2 (11βHSD2) activity was considered the potential targets for wound therapy (Slominski and Zmijewski, 2017). Increased release of cortisol may also lead to delayed re-epithelialization in wound healing (Slominski and Zmijewski, 2017). Another study revealed that stress stimulates the amygdala, which activates the hypothalamus to produce corticotropin-releasing factor (CRF). The latter stimulates proopiomelanocortin-derived adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) production. In the skin, CRF activates proinflammatory mediators such as nuclear factor kappa B, tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α), interleukin-6 (IL-6), andinterleukin-1 (IL-1) (Maarouf et al., 2019). ACTH stimulates the adrenal glands to produce cortisol, the latter suppresses the proinflammatory cytokines IL-1 and TNF-α, increasing inflammation and impairing cutaneous tissue repair (Maarouf et al., 2019). Elevation of cortisol levels decreases the expression of antimicrobial peptides in the epidermis and increases susceptibility to infection (Maarouf et al., 2019). In this study, treatment with C-GQDs significantly reduced cortisol levels in wounded mice.

24 h after the incisional wound, neutrophil levels increased significantly in the untreated, the C-GQDs, and the povidone-iodine groups compared to the control non-injured group. Three days post-wounding, the inflammatory response was significantly increased with a high level of blood neutrophils compared to the control group. An obvious anti-inflammatory effect was observed after C-GQDs application with a significant reduction of neutrophils compared with untreated mice. Povidone iodine application reduced neutrophil levels significantly compared to untreated mice. Analysis of neutrophil levels on days 6 and 9 post-wounding did not reveal significant differences in any of the treatment groups, suggesting that the increased neutrophil levels are part of the acute response to injury (Figure 6S).

Herein, the obtained results are consistent with previous studies showing that inflammatory cells including neutrophils are immediately recruited to the wound site. Neutrophils were significantly elevated over the first 24 h from 106 at 4 h up to a maximum of 5.0 × 106 at 18 h of post-wounding (Kim et al., 2008). During the inflammatory phase, neutrophil infiltration is an early inflammatory reaction needed for the clearance of bacteria and cellular debris in the wound site (Vileikyte, 2007). In addition, neutrophils and activated macrophages are implicated in bacteria phagocytosis and cytokines production which stimulates the growth of fibroblasts (Vileikyte, 2007). The anti-inflammatory effect of GQDs by reducing wound neutrophils was confirmed in another study and no neutrophil infiltration was observed in the skin tissue of a mouse model of a wound dressing with GQDs (Wang, 2020).

The histological analysis revealed that on day 1, the incisional wounds of untreated and C-GQD groups were in the inflammatory phase of wound healing. We observed an increase in the density of inflammatory infiltrate with high infiltration of neutrophils to the wound site (orange stars) (Fig. 10 ). The study also revealed the start of the healing process by increased granulation tissue (black arrows) with a superficial horny layer. On the third day after the incisional wound, we observed disorganized granulation tissue in the untreated group compared with the control non-injured animals. Inflammatory infiltrates were also observed in untreated animal tissues (Fig. 10). After 6 days, the wounds were in the proliferative phase of repair, and the results showed the accelerative effect of C-GQDs on re-epithelization, granulation tissue formation, and complete re-epithelialization in the C-GQDs treated group compared with the control group, untreated (failed re-epithelization and disorganized granulation tissue). On day 9 (Fig. 10), C-GQDs application exhibited complete differentiation of epidermal cells, complete re-epithelization, and the wound was closed (black star) compared to the other groups unclosed on day 9 (control, untreated, and povidone-iodine). The percentage of re-epithelialization was measured in H&E images and indicated an accelerative effect of wound healing ( Figure 7S). Histological analysis showed inflammatory cell infiltration after 1 and 3 days of wounding ( Figure 8S).

Our results showing accelerated healing with-GQDs application are consistent with previous studies. Application of photoactivated erythromycin loaded GQDs on skin wound mice model, showed that the defect granulation tissue was replaced by collagen fibers and complete recovery of the epidermis by 14 days (Wang, 2020). Another study showed that chitosan oligosaccharide functionalized graphene quantum dots (GQDs-COS) can promote healing in bacteria-infected wounds by eliminating inflammation in the wound site (Mei et al., 2020). The same study showed that GQDs-COS reduced the expression of inflammatory markers in the epithelial and stromal skin cells when evaluated by qRT-PCR (Mei et al., 2020).

Wound contraction analysis revealed that C-GQDs significantly accelerated wound closure at 3, 6, and 9 days after injury (all p ≤ 0.001) when compared with the untreated and povidone iodine-treated groups (Fig. 11 ). Wound contraction was also accelerated in the povidone-iodine group when compared to control (p ≤ 0.01). Our in vivo and in vitro results revealed that the C-GQDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility and significantly augmented capability for killing both S. aureus and E. coli with a MIC of 236 µg/mL for both strains compared to a previous study using GQDs prepared using pulsed laser synthesis from benzene as a precursor (900 µg/mL) (Habiba et al., 2015) suggesting a higher efficacy of our C-GQDs preparations.

The effect of GQDs on wound closure was reported by other studies. Polymeric graphene quantum dots (PGQDs) facilitate wound healing via their antibacterial effects by reducing infection and increasing tissue rehydration in the wound site (Huang et al., 2021). Furthermore, in vitro and in vivo results demonstrated that silver nanoparticles coated with GQDs (GQDs@Ag) accelerated the healing of MERSA-infected wounds through their enhanced effect in killing MRSA within the infected wounds (Zhong et al., 2020). Overall, our data show that using a natural source to generate GQDs, the C-GQDs are efficacious in reducing bacterial infection and accelerating wound closure.

In summary, C-GQDs were prepared by a facile microwave-assisted hydrothermal method using cannabis seeds as precursor and without the addition of strong oxidants, thus avoiding the production of toxic gases. The as-prepared C-GQDs were comprehensively characterized using FTIR, UV–vis, Raman spectra, PL, TEM, HRTEM, ζ-potential, and XRD. The C-CQDs synthesized from cannabis seeds precursors were spherical in shape, and their particle size was less than 10 nm. These biomaterials possess negatively charged surfaces and the presence of oxygen-containing functional groups. The obtained C-CQDs in aqueous solution exhibited a blue color when exposed to UV light, and fluorescence experiments demonstrated their excitation-dependent fluorescence emission nature. In this work, we have demonstrated the efficiency of C-GQDs, for the effective inhibition of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. In vitro results revealed the inhibitory efficacy of C-GQDs against different bacterial strains tested such as S. aureus and E. coli. In vivo, we confirmed the in vitro data and showed that C-GQDs exhibited anti-inflammatory activity with decreased neutrophil levels in the blood and dorsal skin tissue. C-GQDs act by accelerating re-epithelization, granulation tissue formation, and wound contraction. In addition, C-GQDs reduced stress, anxiety-like behavior, and decreased cortisol levels. These data support the use of C-GQDs as a therapeutic agent for the treatment of skin wounds.
